title: The Deconstruction of Christianity, Part 3: Suffering and Truth
speaker: Elder Nick Anderson
source_files: 2-2-25 - The Deconstruction of Christianity Part 3 Suffering and Truth - Elder Nick Anderson.txt

outline.title: Crises That Lead to Deconstruction: Suffering
subpoints: Foundations matter — Jesus's parable of the sower: seed on rocky soil has no root and falls away in crisis
Biblical examples who suffered but did not fall away: Job, Joseph, David, Luther, Calvin, Knox, martyrs
American Christianity does not generally expect to suffer, creating fragile faith
Suffering is promised in Scripture — Christians should be ready for it, not shocked by it

outline.title: Crises That Lead to Deconstruction: Doubt, Politics, Purity Culture, the Bible
subpoints: Doubts arise even in faithful believers (Psalm 13:1, Habakkuk); the key is bringing doubts to God
Political crisis: Trump support in 2016 caused some to question their faith
Purity culture in the 1990s-2000s lacked grace for those who failed; students were compared to 'chewed gum'
Crisis of the Bible: claims that it is archaic, contradictory, or immoral (Jephthah's vow cited out of context)
Toxic theology: rejection of penal substitution, hell, complementarianism; original sin called 'abusive'
Abuse: real abuse has occurred, but fallen people are the problem, not Christianity itself

outline.title: Deconstruction Is Not Inevitable
subpoints: Not everyone who goes through suffering, doubt, or political crisis deconstructs
Volumes have been written of people whose faith was strengthened through tremendous suffering
None of these crises is a good reason to reject the faith
When fears, doubts, and sufferings come, we need to root our understanding in the Bible

outline.title: The Fundamental Flaw: Detachment from Objective Truth
subpoints: Francis Schaeffer: the problem is a new view of truth detached from objective and knowable reality
Correspondence theory of truth: something is true because it aligns with reality, regardless of feelings
Lower story (science, facts, logic) vs. upper story (subjective opinion) — the modern mind relegates religion to the upper story
Christianity is objectively true — not just true for believers, but true for everyone everywhere

outline.title: The Authority of Scripture vs. Deconstruction
subpoints: Deconstruction is not the same as Reformation — reformation is 're' (back to original), deconstruction is 'd' (opposite)
Reformers challenged the church with Scripture; deconstructors encourage abandoning Scripture
Westminster Confession 1.5: the Bible evidences itself as the word of God by its matter, doctrine, style, and consistency
Full persuasion of Scripture's infallibility comes from 'the inward work of the Holy Spirit, bearing witness by and with the word'

summary:
Elder Nick Anderson teaches the third lesson in the Deconstruction of Christianity series, focusing on the crises that trigger deconstruction and the deeper issue of objective truth. He begins by observing that virtually every deconstruction story begins with some kind of crisis, and uses the image of an earthquake: some buildings collapse while others remain standing, depending on their foundations.

He surveys six categories of crisis that people commonly cite: suffering (Job, Joseph, and David all suffered greatly yet their faith was strengthened); doubts (the difference between bringing doubts to God vs. conducting an 'exit interview' from faith); political crises (particularly Trump-related disillusionment); purity culture's lack of grace in the 1990s-2000s; crises of the Bible (claims of contradiction and immorality, using Jephthah's vow as an example of cherry-picking out of context); and abuse (real abuse happens, but fallen people are the problem, not Christianity). He argues that none of these constitute good reasons to reject the faith, and that deconstruction is not inevitable even for those who go through them.

The lesson then pivots to what Anderson identifies as the fundamental flaw in deconstruction: the detachment from objective truth. Drawing on Francis Schaeffer's 'two-story' illustration, he argues that the modern mind has relegated religion to the 'upper story' of subjective preference, when in fact Christianity makes objective truth claims. Jesus did not say 'a truth' or 'my truth'; he said 'I am the way, the truth, and the life.' The resurrection is either true or it is not — and if not, 'your faith is also empty' (1 Corinthians 15:14).

Anderson closes by distinguishing Reformation from deconstruction: the 're' means returning to the original standard of Scripture, while the 'd' means doing the opposite — abandoning it. The Westminster Confession's chapter on Scripture is cited to affirm that the Bible's divine authority is ultimately confirmed by 'the inward work of the Holy Spirit, bearing witness by and with the word in our hearts.'
transcript:
## Opening Prayer and Review

This morning for our adult Sunday school, we're going to continue our series on deconstruction. This is the third of five lessons on this topic. And before I start, let's just have a word of prayer. Lord, we thank you for your grace, your goodness, we thank you for your word, and what we learn about you from it. We thank you for the opportunity to learn about the subject of deconstruction. We pray, Lord, that you would fortify our faith as a result of this series; pray that you would help us understand what's going on when people decide to leave the faith, and pray that you would equip us to speak to them and by your grace to bring them back into the fold. In Jesus' name. Amen.

So, I have a lot to get to, so I'm just going to do a drive-by review of what we have gone over. First, the Scripture does warn us that there will be people who fall away from the faith. This started in Genesis 3 with the fall. The idea of deconstruction isn't new, but the word deconstruction originated with a 20th century French philosopher Jacques Derrida. We defined faith deconstruction as a postmodern process of rethinking your faith without regarding Scripture as a standard. There's really no end point to deconstruction. The only common factor among people who are encouraging others to deconstruct is the encouragement to abandon Orthodox Christianity.

Why people are deconstructing: we covered five issues that people have with evangelicalism — a literal reading of the Bible, the belief that women are to be submissive to men, the sanctity of heterosexuality and the rejection of homosexuality, the assumption that the American way of life is best, and identification and partnership with political conservatives, particularly Donald Trump. This isn't new — it goes all the way back to the fall. And deconstruction hurts people.

## Crises That Lead to Deconstruction

Virtually every story of someone who is deconstructing begins with some kind of crisis, some event or events that causes this person to stop and become disillusioned with Christianity. You can have two people who went to the same church, in the same family, had the same pastor, the same youth group, who end up in very different places. And this is very personal for me because that is a description of my brother and I. My brother and I have a few things in common — we both like being miserable; we're Mariners fans. We both enjoy the Seahawks. We both play guitar and we both grew beards. And that's about where it stops. He's agnostic. He grew up in the church. Same church. Same youth groups. Same pastor, same everything. Just 17 months apart. So we experienced all the same things. But he doesn't believe in God. And obviously I'm pretty much the opposite of that.

I think the answer is that foundations are important. Foundations are what we build our life on. And Jesus talks about this in the parable of the sower. He says that some seed goes to the good soil — there are roots and there's fruit that's produced. Others fall on rocky soil and don't have a root. And so they may look for a time like there's good fruit being produced, but when a crisis comes or when suffering and persecution arise, they fall away. When there is an earthquake, sometimes a building falls over and other times buildings aren't fazed. When someone goes through a crisis, there's this earthquake that happens in their life, and either their faith is strengthened or they fall away and topple.

### Suffering

The first crisis is suffering. We have examples of suffering in the Bible. We have Job who went through a tremendous amount of suffering — he lost his kids, his wife told him to curse God and die, he lost his health, then his very unhelpful friends came around. Yet at the end of the day, Job's faith was strengthened going through the suffering. Joseph also went through tremendous suffering — his family betrayed him, left him for dead, sold him into slavery, then the person he was working for's wife betrayed him and got him thrown into prison. Joseph also went through tremendous suffering and yet his faith did not fail. David went through a great deal of suffering — Saul pursued him for years, he experienced the loss of his best friend Jonathan, his son Absalom revolted against him.

We also have examples in history: Luther, who thought he was going to die going before the Diet of Worms. Calvin, his suffering. Knox, Wycliffe, Tyndale, countless other martyrs. And yet they didn't fall away. Why is it that some people hold fast to their faith and others do not? It's because the foundation is either good or the foundation is bad.

Yes, suffering is cited as one of the primary reasons people deconstruct. And I think American Christianity does not in general expect to suffer. I think we have an expectation that life is going to be generally pretty good. We are the most wealthy, most prosperous, most comfortable church in the entire history of the world. And I think what's happened is we've expected that comfort to carry over into our spiritual life. When we stop teaching what the Bible says about suffering, and we don't have the expectation that we are going to suffer, we actually miss people. And so when suffering comes, it's a shock to the system, and some people become disillusioned.

Suffering for Christians is a promise in the Bible. If you get one of those books of promises, there's no section with the heading 'suffering.' It's just not going to sell. But suffering is a promise. We are promised that we are going to suffer. If you are a Christian, you are going to suffer, so we should not be surprised by that.

### Doubt

The second crisis: doubts arise, and doubts arise even in people who are faithful. Psalm 13 begins with David saying, how long, O Lord, will you forget me forever? That's a pretty serious doubt for a believer to have. And David expresses that — he writes that, and that is Scripture. He brought it to God. The disciples were in a boat one time, waves crashing around them, storm raging, and Jesus is down in the boat sleeping. The disciples wake him up: do you even care that we're perishing? They had doubts. And yet they also brought it to Christ.

The difference in these examples and someone who's going through deconstruction is that all of these people brought their fears and their doubts and their tough questions to God. They were not running away from him. Remember that deconstruction is a process of rethinking your faith without regard for the scriptures. Those doubts, those fears — they're dealing with this without bringing it to God.

God doesn't always tell us specifically why we went through those things. At the end of Job, does God take a couple chapters telling him exactly why he went through everything? No, we don't see an explanation given to Job. Romans tells us that we suffer to make us more like Christ. We should be satisfied with that.

### Political Crisis

Another crisis people go through is political crisis. Conservative politics, especially support for Donald Trump in the 2016 election, is a reason why some people have chosen to deconstruct. One person wrote: I was raised as an evangelical charismatic Christian, and I believed that way for 25 years. Everything started to unravel for me in 2016 when evangelicals' outright worship of Trump gave me the motivation to question the faith I held so dearly. Putting so much faith in politics or letting politics have such a strong weight on you that it causes your faith to crumble — there's a lot of things we could say in response to this. There is genuine hurt, genuine confusion there, and we need to be equipped to have these kinds of conversations.

### Purity Culture

Purity culture is another reason people have cited. In the late 90s and early 2000s, the purity culture had some unintended consequences. Teenagers were told to take a pledge of virginity. There's nothing wrong with saying you're going to do that, of course. But it perhaps went a little bit too far: there was not a lot of grace extended to people who failed to keep that pledge. One author wrote that students who weren't virgins were compared to chewed gum, half-eaten lollipops, flowers without petals, or used pieces of tape. And this was traumatizing for people to the point that it led some to deconstruction.

### Crisis of the Bible

Another crisis is a crisis of the Bible. A common sentiment among people who deconstruct is that the Bible is archaic, contradictory, and even immoral. Abraham Piper says basically this — not a direct quote — but he says the Bible contains all kinds of violence and sex that kids shouldn't read, but if they don't read it, they'll go to hell.

One passage commonly cited among people who deconstruct is the vow of Jephthah in Judges chapter 11. He takes this vow that whatever comes out of his house next, he's going to sacrifice to God. And the next thing that comes out of his house is his daughter. And so he sacrifices his daughter. They cite this as God condoning child sacrifice. The problem is that God did not command child sacrifice. This was an unlawful vow that he should not have kept.

People also often cite toxic theology. Lady Gaga helped one person realize that if God made someone gay, then it's okay to be gay because God loves them — forget the idea of repentance from sin, but that's the kind of thinking going through people's minds. People also often cite original sin: 'Jesus died for your sin is psychologically damaging to kids, so it's not worth using.' The problem is that's the heart and soul of the gospel.

Complementarianism is cited as leading to abuse. And yes, there are cases of abuse that have been mishandled by the church. That needs to be dealt with. But saying that Christianity is wrong because sinners are acting like sinners doesn't make sense. Saying that Christianity is wrong because people have sinned against other people is like saying all restaurants are dangerous because you got food poisoning at one once.

## Deconstruction Is Not Inevitable

Deconstruction is not inevitable. Not everybody who goes through suffering or doubts or a problem with Donald Trump or who is taught that the morality the Bible teaches is wrong is going to deconstruct their faith. There are plenty of stories of people, volumes have been written about people who went through tremendous suffering and abuse and yet their faith was stronger for it.

While all of these events can have a great deal of effect on people, none of them are good reasons to reject the faith. The fundamental flaw in deconstruction is really a detachment from objective truth.

## Objective Truth

Francis Schaeffer said that one of the problems with communicating Christianity to a new generation was centered on a new view of truth that detached it from objective and knowable reality. And he said this in the 1970s. It's just continued to work its way into our culture.

What makes something true? I am wearing a shirt. What makes that statement true? The law of identity. Really, what makes it true is that I'm wearing a shirt. You can see my shirt — that is reality. You may not like my shirt. You may think I should be wearing a different shirt. It doesn't matter what you think or feel about my shirt. The reality is I am wearing a shirt. The fancy term for this is correspondence theory of truth — something is true because it aligns with reality, regardless of what you think or feel.

Subjective truth is determined by each individual. This is where we get the phrase 'live your truth' or 'you do you.' This is where people can say, 'well, Christianity is true for you but it's not true for me.' Subjective truth is self-defeating because it says this thing is true while this opposite thing is also true.

Schaeffer talked about a two-story building to help us understand objective versus subjective truth. Lower story truth is science, facts, logic — things that theoretically cannot be denied. Upper story truth is the realm of subjective truth, where I can have my personal opinion about something and you can have yours. They want us to be in that realm where Christianity is not objective truth but subjective truth.

The modern mind has relegated religion to the upper story, which means there really is no more true or false when it comes to religious beliefs and practices. Christianity is objectively true. It's not just true for me. It's true whether you like it or not. Anybody who teaches the Bible faithfully is not giving you their opinion. They are teaching what is objectively true for all people at all times in all locations.

A former atheist wrote: I am not a Christian because it works for me. I had a life prior to Christianity that seemed to be working just fine. And my life as a Christian hasn't always been easy. I'm a Christian because it's true. I'm a Christian because my high regard for the truth leaves me no alternative.

In John 18:37, Pilate says to him, are you a king then? Jesus answered, you say rightly that I am a king. For this cause I was born and for this cause I have come into the world, that I should bear witness to the truth. Everyone who is of the truth hears my voice. Notice that Jesus did not say 'a truth' or 'my truth.' Jesus said in John 14:6, I am the way, the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me.

Jesus made truth claims about himself. In John 8:58, Jesus said to them, most assuredly I say to you, before Abraham was, I am. And then the Jews picked up stones to stone him because they understood that he was saying 'I am God.' He forgave sins — in Matthew 9 he heals a paralytic and tells him your sins are forgiven. Jesus accepted worship — Matthew 14:33 says those in the boat came and worshiped him saying, truly you are the Son of God.

His claim of deity is important because if it was not true, he's either a liar or a lunatic. He was resurrected from the dead. And 1 Corinthians 15:14 says: if Christ is not risen, then our preaching is empty and your faith is also empty.

The heartbeat of our age is that I get to determine my own truth. And this goes all the way back to Genesis 3 when Satan deceived Eve. Now judging someone else's deconstruction is a big no-no because of this belief in self-rule and autonomy. Yet there's a major problem: if you aren't supposed to tell people what to believe about religious truth, they have already contradicted themselves because they definitely don't want you to believe Orthodox Christianity.

## Deconstruction vs. Reformation

Some deconstructors claim that deconstruction is just another expression of reforming. One person has said it is 'a healthy expression of Christian faithfulness. Just ask Martin Luther.' But when we say Reformation, the 're' means back to the original. Deconstruction — the 'd' is the opposite. Reformation is the way forward. Deconstruction is not.

The Reformation phrase Ecclesia reformata, semper reformanda has been traced back to a Dutch reformer, and he wrote: the church is reformed and always being reformed according to the word of God. Those last six words are the key — according to the word of God. Proponents of deconstruction are not encouraging reformation according to the word of God. They're encouraging abandoning the word of God.

Reformation is like taking an old painting that's damaged and restoring it to its original form. Deconstruction is like throwing the painting away and just starting over on your own.

## The Westminster Confession on Scripture

Westminster Confession chapter one, paragraph five: We may be moved and induced by the testimony of the church to an high and reverend esteem of the Holy scriptures. And the heavenliness of the matter, the efficacy of the doctrine, the majesty of the style, the consent of all the parts, the scope of the whole, which is to give all glory to God, and the full discovery it makes of the only way of man's salvation — these and many other incomparable excellencies are arguments whereby it does abundantly evidence itself to be the word of God.

But then it finishes: yet notwithstanding, our full persuasion and assurance of the infallible truth and divine authority thereof is from the inward work of the Holy Spirit, bearing witness by and with the word in our hearts.

We have every rational reason to believe that the Bible really is the word of God. Yet we can only be fully convinced that it really is infallible truth if we are steeped in it. The Holy Spirit is the only one who can convince us and give us infallible assurance that God's word is truly the word of God.

So no — Martin Luther was not a deconstructionist. Calvin was not a deconstructionist. Neither was Zwingli, nor Knox. In those senses of the word they were rethinking faith according to the word of God. In fact, what they were doing was far more difficult — they were reforming the church according to the word of God and the whole world was against them.

The deconstructionist will tell you to abandon God's word and essentially follow your heart. But Luther and others were calling people back to the word of God. That's what we ought to do every day. When you have questions, go to the word of God. If you have doubts, go to the word of God. If you are suffering, go to the word of God.

## Closing Prayer

Our gracious God, we thank you that you are so good to us. We thank you that you truly have spoken and you have made your word known to us. We thank you, Lord, that the Bible is true, that it truly is your word. We thank you that we have it and so many resources to read. We pray, Lord, that you work in our hearts, that we would love your word, that we would be convinced that your word is true, and that we would live our lives according to it. In Christ's name.
